4 min read
1 hour ago

--

If you’ve worked on a React project for a while, you’ve probably faced this situation.
The business team asks for a small change:
“Can we move this field above the address?”
A few days later:
“Can we hide this button for some users?”
Then comes:
“We need another form that’s almost the same, just with two extra fields.”
Before long, you’re creating multiple versions of the same component, adding more if statements, and duplicating code just to support slightly different screens.
I’ve been there too.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One approach that makes these applications much easier to maintain is Config-Based UI.
Instead of writing the UI directly in React, we describe it using a configuration, and React builds the screen for us.
Let’s see how it works.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What is Config-Based UI?
Think of it like giving React a blueprint.
Instead of writing:
<TextInput label="Full Name" />
<Select label="Country" />
<Button>Save</Button>We provide a configuration like this:
{
"components": [
{
"type": "text",
"label": "Full Name"
},
{
"type": "select",
"label": "Country"
},
{
"type": "button",
"text": "Save"
}
]
}React simply reads this configuration and renders the correct components.
The UI becomes data-driven instead of hardcoded.
Why is this useful?
Imagine you have an insurance application with 50 different forms.
Without Config-Based UI:

• Every form needs its own React component.

• Every small change requires a developer.

• Every update needs a new deployment.
With Config-Based UI:

• The frontend stays the same.

• Only the configuration changes.

• New forms can be created much faster.
This saves both development time and maintenance effort.
The Basic Idea
The architecture is actually quite simple.
Configuration (JSON)
↓
React Renderer
↓
React Components
↓
Final UIThe configuration decides what should appear.
React decides how it should appear.
Keeping these responsibilities separate makes the application much easier to scale.
Step 1: Create a Component Registry
The first thing we need is a mapping between a component type and the actual React component.
const componentRegistry = {
text: TextInput,
select: SelectInput,
button: Button
};Now React knows which component should be rendered for each type.
Step 2: Create a Renderer
Now we loop through the configuration.
const Renderer = ({ config }) => {
return (
<>
{config.components.map((item) => {
const Component = componentRegistry[item.type]; if (!Component) return null; return (
<Component
key={item.id}
{...item}
/>
);
})}
</>
);
};That’s it.
Whenever the configuration changes, the UI changes automatically.
Adding a New Component
Suppose tomorrow you need a Date Picker.
Normally you would update multiple files.
With this approach, you simply:

• Create the component.

• Register it.

• Update the configuration.
No renderer changes are needed.
That’s one of the biggest advantages of this pattern.
Dynamic Forms Become Easy
A form can be completely generated from configuration.
{
"type": "text",
"label": "Email",
"required": true
}Tomorrow if the business wants another field, they just add another object to the configuration.
The React code doesn’t change.
Showing or Hiding Components
Sometimes you only want to display a field under certain conditions.
For example:
Show the Company Name field only if the user is employed.
The configuration could look like this:
{
"type": "text",
"visibleWhen": {
"employment": "Working"
}
}The renderer checks this condition before displaying the component.
No extra if statements inside your page components.
Different Users Can See Different Screens
Let’s say an Admin should see a Delete button, but a normal user shouldn’t.
Instead of writing multiple React components, your configuration can define permissions.
{
"type": "button",
"permission": "ADMIN"
}If the user has permission, React renders the button.
Otherwise, it skips it.
Simple.
Layout Can Also Come from Configuration
Configuration doesn’t have to control only components.
It can also define the layout.
{
"layout": {
"columns": 2
}
}Want a one-column layout?
Change the configuration.
Need three columns?
Update the configuration.
No JSX changes required.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Where Does the Configuration Come From?
Usually, it comes from:

• A backend API

• A CMS

• A database

• A JSON file

• A feature management system
This means even non-developers can control parts of the UI without touching the code.
Benefits I Like the Most
After working with Config-Based UI, these are the biggest advantages I’ve noticed:

• Less duplicate code

• Faster feature development

• Easier maintenance

• Better scalability

• Reusable components

• Cleaner React pages

• Easy feature toggles

• Supports multiple products with the same codebase
Things to Keep in Mind
Like every architecture, this one also has a few challenges.
If the configuration becomes too complex, it can be difficult to understand.
That’s why I recommend:

• Keep the configuration simple.

• Validate it before rendering.

• Avoid putting business logic inside JSON.

• Keep React responsible for rendering, not decision-making.
A clean configuration leads to a clean application.
Final Thoughts
Config-Based UI completely changes the way you think about building React applications.
Instead of creating a new component every time the UI changes, you build a flexible rendering engine once and let the configuration decide what users should see.
It’s a pattern that’s especially useful for enterprise applications where requirements change frequently.
The first implementation might take a little extra effort, but once it’s in place, adding new screens becomes much faster — and your React code stays clean, reusable, and easy to maintain.
If you’re building dashboards, admin panels, workflow systems, or dynamic forms, Config-Based UI is definitely worth exploring.
